predictions.title: Cross-cloud agent-identity + MCP registry RFC draft ships by Q3 2026
predictions.body: **By Q3 2026 the Microsoft Agent 365 May 1 GA Registry Sync with AWS Bedrock + Google Cloud Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform + Anthropic MCP SDK ~97M monthly downloads + the parallel Apr 30 OpenAI Agents SDK / Microsoft Foundry Agent Framework 1.0 GA / AWS Bedrock AgentCore stack converges into a published draft RFC (IETF / OpenID Foundation / CSAI Foundation working group) that formalizes the cross-cloud agent-identity + MCP-server inventory + tool-invocation log schema as a standard interop layer.** Forcing function is enterprise procurement: once Microsoft ships the registry-sync primitive at $15/user/mo with M365 admin-surface integration, AWS + Google must either expose parallel surfaces or be absorbed into the Microsoft schema; CSAI's Apr 29 MITRE-CNA authority + ATT&CK v19 named-techniques (MCP server compromise, malicious agent deployment) provide the threat-side standardization pressure; the Apr 30 OpenAI Agents SDK + Microsoft Agent Framework 1.0 GA already standardize the build/runtime layer so the missing piece is the interop-protocol layer the registry-sync proves works; expect Microsoft + AWS + Google to triangulate on a public draft RFC because vendor-specific schemas would lose the M365 + Bedrock + Vertex installed-base anchor advantage. In plain language: Microsoft just made AI agent IDs work across Microsoft, Amazon, and Google clouds — soon a public spec will lock that in. In plain language: In plain language: In plain language:

reasoning.because: Microsoft Agent 365 May 1 GA ships Registry Sync with AWS Bedrock + Google Vertex Gemini Enterprise as a working cross-cloud primitive.
reasoning.given: OpenAI Agents SDK + Foundry Agent Framework 1.0 + AgentCore standardize build / runtime, and CSAI MITRE-CNA + ATT&CK v19 add the threat-side pressure.
reasoning.so_that: AWS, Azure, and Google triangulate on a published draft RFC for agent identity + MCP inventory + tool-invocation logs rather than ceding the schema to one vendor.
reasoning.landing: By Q3 2026; IETF / OpenID Foundation / CSAI working-group draft RFC.
reasoning.plain_language: Microsoft just made AI agent IDs work across Microsoft, Amazon, and Google clouds; the three of them will soon publish a shared spec instead of fighting over it.

predictions.summary: Microsoft Agent 365 GA Registry Sync with AWS Bedrock + Google Vertex, the Apr 30 OpenAI Agents SDK + Foundry Agent Framework 1.0, and CSAI MITRE-CNA pressure converge on a published interop layer. By Q3 2026 an IETF / OpenID / CSAI draft RFC for cross-cloud agent identity + MCP inventory + tool-invocation logs is expected to ship.

predictions.title: Frontier cyber-AI models become US export-control category by Q3 2026
predictions.body: **By Q3 2026 the Apr 7 Anthropic Mythos Preview launch under Project Glasswing (~50 launch partners + ~40 critical-software-infrastructure orgs + $100M usage credits + $4M open-source security donations) + the Apr 30 Bloomberg-disclosed NSA Project Aether benchmarking Mythos against classified sovereign cyber tools + the May 1 Pentagon CTO statement separating the Anthropic blacklist from the NSA Mythos engagement creates the regulatory-pull for the US to formally define a frontier cyber-capability AI export-control category — at least one government issuance (BIS Commerce Department export-control rule under Cat 5 part 2 dual-use cryptography or a parallel cyber-capability category, NIST AI Cyber Center RFI follow-up, or White House executive order) explicitly names frontier cyber-AI as a license-required category; at least two other foundation labs (OpenAI, Google DeepMind, xAI, Mistral, or Cohere) ship a comparable gated-distribution program with public partner-list disclosure mirroring the Glasswing pattern.** Forcing function is the asymmetry the Aether disclosure surfaces: the same model the Pentagon refused to procure is the one the NSA depends on for sovereign cyber benchmarking, which makes it impossible for the executive branch to leave the regulatory category undefined; the Apr 29 CSAI Foundation MITRE-CNA authority + ATT&CK v19 named-techniques (MCP server compromise, tool invocation abuse) provide the disclosure-side regulatory infrastructure. The path of least resistance is BIS issuing a rule under Cat 5 because it has 30 years of dual-use-tech precedent and avoids carving up the full FDA-style approval regime that would slow open-source agent skills. In plain language: When the NSA started borrowing Anthropic's most powerful AI to find Microsoft bugs, the US government had to start regulating who else can borrow it. In plain language: In plain language: In plain language:

reasoning.because: Bloomberg Apr 30 disclosed NSA Project Aether benchmarks Anthropic Mythos against classified sovereign cyber tools while the Pentagon excludes Anthropic from procurement.
reasoning.given: Mythos launched gated under Project Glasswing with ~50 partners, $100M credits, and $4M open-source donations, and CSAI MITRE-CNA + ATT&CK v19 supply the disclosure infrastructure.
reasoning.so_that: The executive branch defines a frontier cyber-capability category, and at least two more foundation labs publish Glasswing-style gated programs.
reasoning.landing: By Q3 2026; one BIS rule, NIST AI Cyber Center follow-up, or executive order plus two parallel gated programs.
reasoning.plain_language: The NSA is using Anthropic's most powerful AI to hunt Microsoft bugs while the Pentagon refuses to buy it; the government will turn that contradiction into an export-license category.

predictions.summary: Apr 7 Mythos Preview under Project Glasswing, Apr 30 Bloomberg disclosure of NSA Project Aether benchmarking Mythos, and the May 1 Pentagon CTO carve-out compose a regulatory pull-forward. By Q3 2026 a BIS, NIST, or executive issuance is expected to name frontier cyber-AI as license-required, with two more labs shipping gated-distribution programs.

predictions.title: SEC publishes AI-revenue disclosure concept release by Q4 2026
predictions.body: **By Q4 2026 the cohort buyside-pressure stack — Apr 30 AAPL Q3 guide +14-17% (vs 9.5% consensus) printed without a separate AI-services line + $700B 2026 Big-Tech AI capex tape + AAPL +$100B buyback authorization + Apr 29 MSFT $37B AI-run-rate single-line-item disclosure precedent + OpenAI $25B annualized + $2B/month run rate + 122M paid by 2026 with $8/month + ad pilot $100M-annualized opacity + Cerebras IPO mid-May at $22-25B targeted — forces the SEC to publish a concept release (Reg S-K amendment proposal) on audited AI-business KPI disclosure for hyperscalers and foundation labs filing public comparables; at least one Big-3 hyperscaler pre-emptively publishes an audited AI-business KPI segment in a 10-Q footnote, ahead of any rule finalization, to anchor the disclosure shape.** Forcing function is the disclosure-shape gap between the cohort's collective $700B capex commitment and the cohort's lack of audited AI-KPI footnote: the buyside is being asked to underwrite eight digits of AI capex per quarter without the separate-segment view the SEC already requires for cloud, gaming, and other reporting segments. The May 1 Cerebras IPO roadshow with public S-1 disclosure + the Apr 28 WSJ OpenAI-revenue tape + the Apr 29 MSFT $37B precedent are the operative anchor signals; SEC has already opened the regulatory window via the Apr 28 Reg S-K modernization comment period (companion to climate-disclosure rulemaking), and AI-revenue disclosure is the natural next addition. In plain language: Investors are spending hundreds of billions on AI without seeing what they're getting back — the SEC will publish a draft rule asking companies to break it out. In plain language: In plain language: In plain language:

reasoning.because: AAPL Q3 guide +14-17% printed without an AI-services line, MSFT cited a $37B AI run-rate, and the cohort's 2026 capex tape ran to ~$700B.
reasoning.given: Cerebras IPO S-1 disclosure mid-May, the Apr 28 WSJ OpenAI-revenue tape, and the Apr 28 Reg S-K modernization comment period open the regulatory window.
reasoning.so_that: The SEC issues a concept release on audited AI-business KPI disclosure and at least one Big-3 hyperscaler ships a pre-emptive 10-Q AI-segment footnote.
reasoning.landing: By Q4 2026; concept release plus one hyperscaler 10-Q AI-KPI footnote.
reasoning.plain_language: Hundreds of billions are flowing into AI with no separate line on the financials; the SEC will draft a rule that forces companies to break out what AI brings in.

predictions.summary: AAPL's $700B-capex-cohort guide without an AI-services line, MSFT's $37B AI run-rate precedent, the Apr 28 WSJ OpenAI tape, and the Cerebras IPO S-1 sit on the Apr 28 Reg S-K modernization window. By Q4 2026 an SEC concept release is expected on audited AI-KPI disclosure with one Big-3 hyperscaler pre-emptively publishing an AI-segment footnote.

predictions.title: Mag-7 physical-AI capex segments split out in a 10-Q footnote by H2 2026
predictions.body: **By H2 2026 the Tesla Q2 2026 Fremont Optimus production-line build-out (legacy Model S + Model X lines being phased out for a 1M-units/year first-generation robotics plant; Optimus 3 launches summer 2026 at $20K-$30K with hundreds of units in 2026 ramping to thousands then tens of thousands by 2027-2028) + the Apr 28 Apptronik + Jabil Apollo manufacturing partnership + Figure 03 production at 1/hour with overnight fully-autonomous runs + Tesla CFO commentary on capex-allocation between EV and robotics tape will force at least one Mag-7 (Tesla initially; ≥1 sibling — NVIDIA Isaac, Amazon Robotics, Microsoft) to publish physical-AI capex as a separate segment or footnote in a 10-Q ahead of any SEC mandate, mirroring how cloud-segment disclosure was first published voluntarily in 2014-2015 before the SEC formalized it.** Forcing function is the disclosure-precedent expansion: cloud capex segmentation became standard once the dollar amounts crossed a buyside-attention threshold; physical-AI capex at Tesla's 1M-units/year nameplate (~$25B-$35B annualized at the price target) crosses that threshold in a single facility, layered on Apptronik + Figure + Boston Dynamics commercial deployment trajectory. The Q2 production-line build-out plus the Optimus 3 summer launch mean Tesla 10-Q for the period ending Sept 30 2026 is the natural first disclosure venue; competitive pressure from a single Tesla footnote disclosure will pull in a sibling Mag-7 disclosure within one or two filing cycles. In plain language: Tesla is spending big to make a million robots a year — soon Wall Street will demand to see those costs broken out separately. In plain language: In plain language: In plain language:

reasoning.because: Tesla starts the Fremont 1M-units/year Optimus line in Q2 2026 with Optimus 3 launching summer 2026 at $20K-$30K.
reasoning.given: Apptronik + Jabil Apollo, Figure 03 at 1/hour with overnight autonomous runs, and Boston Dynamics make humanoid-robotics capex a buyside category.
reasoning.so_that: At least one Mag-7 (Tesla first; NVIDIA Isaac, Amazon Robotics, or Microsoft to follow) publishes physical-AI capex as a separate 10-Q segment or footnote ahead of any SEC mandate.
reasoning.landing: By H2 2026; Tesla 10-Q for the period ending Sept 30 2026 is the natural first disclosure venue.
reasoning.plain_language: Tesla is spending billions to make a million robots a year, so its 10-Q has to break out humanoid-robot costs and the rest of Big Tech will copy it.

predictions.summary: Tesla's Q2 Fremont Optimus build-out at 1M-units/year nameplate, Apptronik + Jabil Apollo, and Figure 03 at 1/hour cross the buyside-attention threshold that drove cloud-segment disclosure in 2014-2015. By H2 2026 at least one Mag-7 is expected to break out physical-AI capex as a 10-Q segment or footnote, with Tesla's Sept 30 filing the natural first venue.
